name: Surface Code

threshold.notes: Depolarizing threshold from Dennis et al. (2002) via stat-mech mapping. Circuit-level threshold from Raussendorf & Harrington (2007) with standard depolarizing noise.

connectivity: 2D nearest-neighbor grid with periodic or open boundaries
description: The surface code is a topological stabilizer code defined on a 2D lattice of qubits. It achieves high error thresholds due to local stabilizer checks and is a leading candidate for fault-tolerant quantum computing with superconducting architectures. The code encodes one logical qubit per planar patch and supports transversal CNOT via lattice surgery.

name: Rotated Surface Code

threshold.notes: Same thresholds as the standard surface code; the rotation reduces qubit count without changing the code's error-correcting properties under standard noise.

key_papers.title: Surface codes: Towards practical large-scale quantum computation
Topological and subsystem codes on low-degree graphs with flag qubits

description: The rotated surface code is a more qubit-efficient variant of the standard surface code, requiring only d^2 physical qubits instead of 2d^2 - 1 for distance d. It achieves this by rotating the lattice by 45 degrees, placing data qubits at vertices of a tilted grid. Error-correction properties and thresholds are essentially identical to the standard surface code.

name: Color Code

threshold.notes: Depolarizing threshold from Bombin & Martin-Delgado (2006). Circuit-level threshold from Landahl et al. (2011) for the 4.8.8 color code.

key_papers.title: Fault-tolerant quantum computing with color codes

description: Color codes are topological stabilizer codes defined on trivalent lattices with 3-colorable faces. They support a transversal implementation of the full Clifford group, which is an advantage over surface codes. The 4.8.8 color code is the most commonly studied variant, offering good thresholds with moderate qubit overhead.

name: Steane [[7,1,3]] Code

threshold.notes: Circuit-level threshold from Aliferis & Cross (2007) using flag-based fault-tolerant syndrome extraction. No meaningful depolarizing threshold for a single block code.

key_papers.title: Error correcting codes in quantum theory
Multiple-particle interference and quantum error correction

description: The Steane code is the smallest CSS code, encoding one logical qubit into seven physical qubits with distance 3. It is based on the classical [7,4,3] Hamming code and supports the full transversal Clifford group. It is widely used in pedagogical contexts and as a building block for concatenated fault-tolerant schemes.

name: Shor [[9,1,3]] Code

threshold.notes: The Shor code was the first QEC code demonstrated and is primarily of historical significance. Practical threshold values are not typically quoted for it.

connectivity: Groups of 3 qubits in blocks of 3 (concatenated structure)

key_papers.title: Scheme for reducing decoherence in quantum computer memory

description: The Shor code is the first quantum error-correcting code, encoding one logical qubit into nine physical qubits. It uses a concatenation of a 3-qubit bit-flip code and a 3-qubit phase-flip code. While not efficient by modern standards, it is foundational to the field and is used extensively in introductory quantum error correction.

name: Bacon-Shor Code

threshold.notes: Circuit-level threshold from Aliferis & Cross (2007). The threshold depends on the specific m x m geometry used.

connectivity: 2D grid with row and column parity checks

key_papers.title: Operator quantum error-correcting subsystems for self-correcting quantum memories
Fault-tolerant quantum computation against biased noise

description: The Bacon-Shor code is a subsystem code defined on an m x m grid of qubits. It uses gauge operators to simplify syndrome extraction, requiring only 2-qubit measurements instead of higher-weight stabilizer checks. It is particularly well-suited for biased noise models and offers a simpler path to fault tolerance than full stabilizer codes.

name: Repetition Code

threshold.notes: Circuit-level threshold for the repetition code under circuit-level depolarizing noise. Only corrects one type of error (bit-flip or phase-flip), not both.

key_papers.title: Suppressing quantum errors by scaling a surface code logical qubit
Realizing repeated quantum error correction in a distance-three surface code

description: The repetition code is the simplest quantum error-correcting code, encoding one logical qubit into n physical qubits arranged in a 1D chain. It can only correct one type of Pauli error (bit-flip or phase-flip), making it a classical code embedded in a quantum setting. It is widely used for benchmarking hardware and decoders due to its simplicity.

name: Gross [[144,12,12]] qLDPC Code

threshold.notes: Thresholds for qLDPC codes are an active area of research. Bravyi et al. (2024) demonstrated that this code achieves a logical error rate below physical error rate at practical noise levels.

connectivity: Non-local connectivity required; bivariate bicycle structure with weight-6 checks

key_papers.title: Quantum error correction below the surface code threshold
High-threshold and low-overhead fault-tolerant quantum memory

description: The Gross [[144,12,12]] code is a bivariate bicycle quantum LDPC code that encodes 12 logical qubits into 144 physical qubits with distance 12. It achieves a high encoding rate (k/n = 1/12) compared to surface codes. This code demonstrated that qLDPC codes can outperform surface codes in logical error rate per physical qubit, marking a significant milestone for practical quantum error correction.

name: Toric Code

threshold.notes: Depolarizing threshold from Dennis et al. (2002). Circuit-level threshold from Wang et al. (2003). The toric code has periodic boundary conditions yielding two logical qubits.

connectivity: 2D nearest-neighbor grid with periodic boundary conditions (torus)

key_papers.title: Confinement-Higgs transition in a disordered gauge theory and the accuracy threshold for quantum memory

description: The toric code is the original topological quantum error-correcting code, defined on a torus (2D lattice with periodic boundary conditions). It encodes two logical qubits and has been foundational in establishing the connection between quantum error correction and topological order. The surface code is its planar variant with open boundaries.
